generations.text:
**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the flicker of the lighthouse lamp casting long shadows upon the paper, and the wind howling like a wounded beast in the distance. The sea, that restless and unrelenting thing, is rising in fury, and I fear it may claim me before dawn. Yet I must speak, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear I may never again.

You would not recognize me, I think. The years have worn me like the salt upon the stone. My hands are rough, my back bent, and my hair, once dark, now streaked with silver. But my heart—ah, my heart still beats for you, though I have not let it show for too long.

Five years. That is how long it has been since you left. I remember the day as if it were yesterday. You stood at the door, your face pale, your eyes filled with tears that you would not let fall. You said you could not stay, that you could not bear the weight of what we had become. I did not argue, for I knew you were right. And so you went, and I was left with the sea, the lamp, and the silence.

I have kept the lamp burning, as I always have, for it is more than a tool—it is a promise. A promise to those who sail the dark waters, to the ones who may not see the shore if the

**My Dearest Eliza,**

It is with a heart heavy with regret and a mind clouded by the storm’s approach that I take up my quill. Five long years have passed since your last letter, and though time has worn the edges of my once-vigorous frame, it has not dulled the ache of your absence. I have often wondered if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if the memory of your father has faded like the fading light of a distant lighthouse on a stormy night.

I write to you now not as a man of the sea, but as a man who has spent his twilight years alone, watching the waves crash against these rocks with a sorrow that no tide could ever wash away. The storm that now gathers on the horizon is unlike any I have seen in my years here. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, rages with a fury I fear may be beyond its usual bounds.

I have spent my life standing guard over these cliffs, ensuring that no soul is lost to the dark. But tonight, I find myself guarding something far more fragile—my own soul. I have not spoken to you in years, and I do not know if my words will reach you, or if you will even read this. But if the wind should carry it, if the waves should carry it, I must say what I have long held in my heart.

I have

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee and the sea raging as though it seeks to swallow the very cliffs that have stood guard over these shores for generations. The lighthouse, old and steadfast, trembles in its bones, and I fear the night may yet claim it. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as I sit here with the lamp in my hands and the storm pressing in from all sides, I find myself thinking of you more than ever.

You were a child when you left, and I do not blame you for wanting to chase the world beyond this rock and its ceaseless waves. I only wish I had understood then, as I did not, that you were not running from me, but from the shadow I cast. I was a man of stone and silence, and you, my dear, were the light that should have guided me.

I have kept the lighthouse, as I have kept my vows. The sea has always been my companion, and I have always been its keeper. But tonight, I am not so sure I can keep the light burning. The storm is unlike any I have seen in my years here, and the wind has carried with it a voice I have not heard in five years — a voice that once called me "Dad." I have not spoken it in years, but I feel it on my lips, and I wonder if you would still call me that

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee through the narrow cracks of this old lighthouse. The sea roars, and the sky is a bruised shade of black, as though the heavens themselves are weeping. I’ve spent most of the evening pacing the lantern room, my back to the storm, trying to steady my breath and my thoughts. I fear I may not make it through the night, and if I do, I fear I may not have the strength to write this letter properly. But I must, for you.

Five years have passed since the last time I saw you, and I have not spoken a word to you since. I have not written, nor have I sent a single letter. You were angry, I know. You called me a fool, a man who clung to the past like a drowning man to driftwood. And I suppose, in a way, you were right. I have been a fool, Eleanor. I have let this lighthouse consume me, let the sea and the silence take the place of the life I once had.

I have not seen your mother since the day she left. I have not seen you either. I have not even dared to dream of you. And yet, tonight, as the storm gathers, I feel something stir within me—something I have not felt in years. Perhaps it is the wind, perhaps it is the sea, or perhaps

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee at my window, the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very cliffs that cradle this lighthouse. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. But I must write, for I know you are reading this, even if you do not believe it.

Five years have passed since you left, and five years have not softened the ache in my heart. You were once my light in the darkness, my little star, and now you are but a shadow in my memory. I have not heard from you, nor have I dared to write, for fear that the words might not reach you—or worse, that they might be the last I ever speak to you.

You were a child when you ran away, Eleanor. A child with a fire in her soul and a heart too big for the world. I tried to hold you close, to shield you from the cruel winds of life, but you were always more like the sea—wild, untamed, and free. You left with your own dreams, and I, in my folly, let you go. I told myself I would wait, that I would be here when you returned. But the years have worn me down, and the silence has grown heavy.

I have kept the lighthouse as you once asked, and the light still burns through the storm. It is the only

**The Lighthouse, East Cliff**  
**October 12th, 1897**  

My Dearest Eliza,  

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my daughter, my only child.  

I know you do not want to hear from me. You have made that quite clear. But I must say this: I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the day you left, nor the way you walked away from me, your face set like stone, your eyes cold as the sea. I have not forgotten the way your mother’s voice broke when she told me you were going to London to study music. I have not forgotten the silence that followed.  

I have not forgotten the nights I sat by the light, watching the waves crash against the rocks, wondering if I had done something to drive you away. I have not forgotten the way my hands shook when I lit the lamp for the first time after you left. I have not forgotten the loneliness that settled in my bones like a frost.  

But tonight, as the storm rises, I feel the weight of it all pressing down upon me. The lighthouse is old

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, in the dim glow of the lighthouse, with the wind howling like a banshee and the sea roaring as if it would swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn. Yet, I have been given a rare moment of clarity—this night, I think of you.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left this house, that once stood as a beacon not just for ships, but for your soul. I have not spoken to you since that last letter, and I know you have not written to me. I suppose we both have our reasons. I would not blame you for what you have done, though I do not forgive you. You were young, and perhaps I was too harsh. But I was a father, and I did what I thought was right.

I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have. The light still burns, though it has grown dimmer with the years. I have watched the waves crash against the rocks, and I have listened to the silence between them. It is a silence that echoes with the absence of your voice.

I have thought of you often, though I have tried not to. You were my daughter, and I was your father. But the world has a way of making even the closest of kin strangers. I have seen your name in the town papers—once or twice, I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not. I write with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence, yet I cannot let this night pass without speaking to you, for the storm that rages outside is not the only thing that threatens my soul.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken to you in that time. I have not heard your voice, nor seen your face, nor felt the warmth of your presence. You were my light, my guiding star, and now I am left in darkness, both within and without.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years, and I know the power of light. It cuts through the storm, it guides the lost, and it gives hope to those who sail the dark waters. But I have not been able to shine that light for you. I have not been able to reach out, to apologize, to explain. I have been a fool, Eleanor, and I have let my pride blind me to the pain I caused you.

I do not know if you will read this, or if you will even believe the words that come from a man who has been too afraid to reach out. But I write this now, in the stillness before the storm, knowing that if I do not speak, I may never have the chance again.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles not from the storm outside, but from the weight of the silence that has stretched between us like the long, dark hours of a midnight watch. Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years since you left this house, and me, behind. I have not heard your voice in all that time. I have not seen your face, nor felt the warmth of your presence in this cold, wind-swept tower. The sea has been a constant companion, but you have been a ghost.

Tonight, the storm is coming. It is not like the others. The sky has been dark for days, and the wind howls as if it carries the voices of the dead. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I have watched the waves crash against the rocks below like the pounding of a heart. It is a cruel and lonely sound. I have not spoken to another soul in weeks, save for the occasional sailor who dares to brave the sea in this weather. They say the lighthouse is cursed. I have no wish to be remembered as such.

But I fear the storm may not be the end of me. I fear it is the end of us. I have not written to you, nor have I tried to reach you. I do not know if you still live in that town you left, or if you have moved on to something better. I do not know

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea has grown restless, and the storm that now gathers on the horizon is no mere squall—it is a beast of the deep, and I fear it may claim me before dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of silence, of watching the beacon burn through the dark, and of wondering if the light I cast upon the waves could ever reach you. I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have, but I have not kept your name. I have not kept you. And yet, tonight, as the sky darkens and the waves rise, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years.

I have often wondered if you remember the nights we spent together, when you were a child, and I would read you tales of sailors and sea monsters, of lost ships and ghostly lights. You used to call me "Captain" when you were little, and I would pretend to be a mariner of the stars. I never thought such a thing would one day be a memory.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I know you have not spoken to me. I do not know if you blame me for what happened, or if you simply chose to forget. But I must say this now, before the storm swallows me whole: I am not the man I was when

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the lantern’s glow flickering in the wind, the sea howling like a beast in torment. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may claim me before the dawn. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not written a single word to you in all that time. Yet now, as the heavens weep and the waves crash like the sorrow in my heart, I find myself compelled to reach out once more.

You were once my light, Eliza. Not the lantern that guards this rock, but the warmth in my soul, the softness in my old bones, the reason I kept the light burning through the long, lonely nights. But time and distance have dimmed that light, and I have let the sea take the place of your voice in my ears.

I know you have not forgiven me. I know you have not forgotten the day you left. I should not expect you to forgive me, for I have done nothing to deserve it. I have been a fool, a man too blind to see the pain I caused you. I thought I was protecting you, that the world was too harsh, too cruel. I thought I could shield you from the storm, but I only made it worse.

You were a child when you left, and I was a man who had forgotten how to love. I told myself I was doing what was right, that I was keeping you safe

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time I saw you, and the world has changed in ways I never could have foreseen. The sea, that old and faithful companion, has grown more restless, and tonight, it is said that a storm of terrible fury is coming. I do not know if I shall see dawn.

You must not think I have forgotten you, though I have not spoken your name in so long. I have not forgotten the days we shared, nor the love that once bound us. I have only been silent because the silence has been the only way I could bear the weight of what I had to say. I could not bring myself to speak it, not even to myself.

You were always so young, Eliza, and I was too proud to admit that I had made a mistake. I thought I was protecting you, that I was doing what was best for you. But I was wrong. I was blind. I let my own fears and pride blind me to the truth—that you were not a child to be kept from the world, but a woman with her own soul, her own dreams, her own right to choose her path.

I have spent these years alone, watching the sea and the stars, and I have come to understand the loneliness of a man who has lost his daughter. I have learned that silence is not always a sign

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the narrow cracks of this old lighthouse. The sea roars and the sky is black as ink, and I fear the storm may take more than just the light from the tower. I’ve not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as I sit here, I feel the weight of silence pressing upon me like the tide.

You were a child when you left, and I did not stop you. You were young, full of fire and fury, and I, a man of stone and salt. I thought I could protect you, but I only became a barrier. I did not understand the world you were growing into, nor the heart you were building. I was a lighthouse keeper, not a father. I kept the light burning, but I let the darkness creep into my soul.

I have heard from the postman once or twice, and I’ve read your letters. They were sharp, like the edge of a blade. You spoke of your own life, of the world beyond this rocky coast, and I listened with a heart that had long since grown cold. I did not reply, for I feared what words might bring. I feared the truth, and I feared what you might think of me.

But tonight, as the storm gathers, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years. I have no one else to speak to,

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the lighthouse walls, and the sea roiling as though it would swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may not end with the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have done little to soften the ache of your absence. I have watched the tides come and go, the moon wax and wane, and the stars blink out one by one, yet still I have not seen your face in the darkness. You were always the light, Eleanor—bright, sharp, and full of fire. I should have known you would not stay in the shadows.

I do not write this to beg for your return, for I know you are not the same woman who left. You were a child then, full of dreams and fury, and I, a man who had long since lost my way. I was a fool to think I could hold you back, or that I could ever be the father you deserved. I was a lighthouse keeper, not a father, and I failed you.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the weight of that failure like a stone in my chest. I have always been a man of duty, of service to the sea and the ships that pass by. But this—this is not duty. This is fear. Fear that I may not

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you far from the comfort of home. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I find myself longing for the days when our conversations were filled with laughter and the scent of your mother's lavender soap. The silence between us has grown as vast as the sea, and I wonder if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if it has been lost to the winds that howl through these lonely stones.

Tonight, a storm is coming—dark and furious, as though the very heavens are weeping. I have seen such tempests before, and I know their fury. The waves will rise like mountains, and the lighthouse will stand as a sentinel against the chaos. I have prepared the lamp, lit the oil, and set the beacon to glow with unwavering light. It is my duty, and I will not falter.

But I write this not to boast of my steadfastness, but to confess that I have been thinking of you. I have often looked out at the sea, wondering if you still look upon it with the same wonder that once filled your eyes. I remember the day you first came to visit, when you were but a child, and you ran up the spiral stairs, laughing as the wind tugged at your dress. You were a bright light in a world that had grown dim for me.

I know the years have changed you

**The Lighthouse, Skerry Point**  
**October 12th, 1894**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write this with a hand that trembles not from the cold, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years, Eleanor. Five long, silent years. I have not spoken your name in all that time, and yet it lingers in my mind like the echo of a storm on the rocks.  

You were but a girl when you left, and I, a man who had long since lost his way. I remember the day you stood at the door, your eyes like the sea at dawn—clear, but distant. You said you had to go, that you needed to see the world beyond these cliffs. I did not argue, for I knew the sea had always called to you more than it called to me. I only said, “Come back when you’re ready, my child.”  

And you did not.  

I have been alone here since. The lighthouse is my only companion, and the wind my only voice. The sea is a cruel mistress, and I have learned to fear her more than I feared the darkness. But tonight—tonight, the storm is coming. A beast of a storm, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I have seen such tempests before, and I know what they bring. The waves will rise, the light will flicker, and if the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the windows of this lonely keep. The sea roars in protest, and the sky, once a canvas of solemn gray, now churns with the fury of a thousand vengeful spirits. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write, not to mend what has been broken, but to leave something behind—something that might one day reach you.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with fire in her eyes and a heart too proud to ask for what she needed. I remember the day you stood at the door, your face pale, your hands clenched around the hem of your coat, as if it might hold you together. You said you could not stay, that you could not bear the silence, the weight of the sea, the loneliness of this place. You left, and I did not stop you. I thought, perhaps, that the sea would take you, or the cold, or the years. But you did not come back.

I have watched the years pass like the tides, relentless and indifferent. The lighthouse stands as it always has, a sentinel against the dark, but I have grown old in its shadow. The light burns still, though I have grown weary of its duty. I have seen many storms, but none like

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a hand that trembles not from the cold, but from the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long years since you left this house, and five years since I last saw your face. The sea has not been kind to me, nor has the silence of this place. But tonight—tonight is different. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take me before I can say what I must.

You were a child when you left, but I have felt the passage of time as if it were a tide that pulled me further from the shore of our lives. I have not seen your face in the mirror since that day you walked out, and yet I see it in the mist that rolls in from the sea. I see it in the lantern's glow, in the creak of the stairs, in the wind that howls through the cracks of this old stone.

I have kept the light burning for you, Eleanor. I have kept it burning for all who sail these waters, for the lost, the lonely, the ones who have nowhere else to go. But I have not kept it burning for myself. I have not kept it burning for the love that once was, and that now lies buried in the sand.

You were angry. I understand that now. You were angry at the way I spoke, at the way I kept my thoughts to myself, at the way I let the sea

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the flicker of a dying flame, the wind howling like the devil himself outside these ancient stone walls. The sea roars as though it would swallow the sky whole, and I find myself wondering—will I be the one who is swallowed? Or will I be the one who survives, only to find that the world has moved on without me?

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since the last time I set eyes on you. I have not spoken to you, nor have you spoken to me. You sent me a letter once, sealed with your mother’s seal, but I did not open it. I could not. The sight of your name, the ink of your hand, it was too much.

I know what you think of me. That I was a failure. That I chose the lighthouse over you. That I let the sea take my wife and my son, and then let the sea take my peace as well. But you do not understand, child. I did not choose it. I was chosen. The lighthouse is my burden, my calling, my curse. And I have borne it for more than thirty years.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The sky is black as ink, the waves are like the claws of some great beast, and the wind sings a song of death. I have lit the lamp, as I have done every night for thirty years, but I wonder if

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart full of sorrow and longing. Five long years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and yet it feels like only yesterday. The sea has been a faithful companion these many years, but it is not the sea I have been thinking of tonight. It is you.

The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves rise like mountains in the dark. I have seen storms before, but this one—this one feels different. The sky is black as ink, and the wind sings a mournful tune that stirs something deep within me. I fear for my life, Eliza, and yet I fear more for the life I have lost.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I ever will again. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write. Perhaps it is the sea, or perhaps it is the weight of silence pressing upon my soul. I cannot bear the silence any longer.

I have always believed that the sea reveals the truth of a man. It does not lie, and it does not forgive. I have spent my life watching the horizon, searching for something that might bring me peace. But I have found only the cold, indifferent waves. And yet, I have not turned away from my duty. I have stood guard over this lonely tower, night after night, year after year.

You were once

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of this old tower. The sea roars in protest, as if it too knows the weight of the silence that has stretched between us for five long years. I have not spoken to you since the day you left, and I do not know if I will ever speak to you again. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the lantern’s light flickers in the dark, I feel the need to say something—anything—to you.

You were once my pride, my light in the darkness of this lonely life. I remember the day you were born, Eleanor, as if it were yesterday. You were the first thing I ever truly loved, the reason I chose this life of solitude. I gave up the world for you, and for a time, it was worth it. But then you grew, and you changed. You became distant, and I became a stranger to you.

I do not know what I did to make you turn your back on me, but I wish I could undo it. I wish I could take back the words I said in anger, the times I was too afraid to reach for you. I know I have been a poor father, a man who let pride and fear stand in the way of love. But I never stopped loving you, Eleanor. Not once.

Tonight, as the sky darkens and the waves rise

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old tower. The sea, that restless thing, roars with a fury I have not heard in years. I fear the night may not end with the sun rising again. But if it does, I hope you will read these words.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have spent each of them in silence, save for the creak of the lighthouse and the moan of the waves. You were a child then, and I a man who thought he had all the time in the world. I was wrong. Time does not wait for those who stand at the edge of the world, watching the horizon.

I have not written to you, nor have I sent a single letter, and I do not know if you will ever receive this. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the weight of my years pressing down upon me. I have been a fool, Eleanor. A man who let pride and fear keep him from reaching out. I have not been the father you needed, nor the man you deserved.

I remember the day you left. You were only twelve, and I was too stubborn to see the pain in your eyes. You told me you wished to see the world, to live your own life, and I said, “You will not be safe out there.” I was wrong again.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long, bitter years since I last saw you, and though the sea has been my constant companion, it has never felt so lonely as the silence of your absence. I write this on a night that has grown colder than any I've known, the wind howling like a wounded beast through the cracks of this old tower. The storm is upon us, and I fear the sea may take more than just the light from my lantern tonight.

You were always a child of the sea, Eleanor, as I was. You would run to the shore with your bare feet, chasing the tides, your laughter echoing across the dunes like the sound of waves on a summer’s day. But time, like the tides, has carried you away. I have heard from your mother, though I do not know how she finds the strength to write to me, or if she writes at all. She said you are married now, or so she claims. I have no wish to believe it, but I am old, and the world changes faster than the light I keep burning.

I have kept the lighthouse for over thirty years, and though I have seen many storms, none have ever made me feel so small. Tonight is different. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza. Not a single word. I have not sent a letter, nor have I dared to call. The silence has grown like the sea itself—vast, unyielding, and ever-present. But tonight, as the wind howls like a beast from the depths and the sky darkens with the promise of a storm that will not relent, I find myself compelled to reach out once more.

You were a child when you left. A child with fire in her eyes and a heart too proud to ask for anything. You said you were going to find your own way, and I let you go. I thought it was for the best. I thought I was protecting you. But I was wrong, Eliza. I was wrong in so many ways.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and I have not heard a word from you. I suppose it is better this way—better that I should not have to bear the weight of your silence. Yet, as the tempest gathers strength, I find myself wondering if the sea, too, has its own kind of silence.

You were always a spirited girl, Eleanor. I remember the day you first climbed the tower, so young and full of questions. "Why do you light the lamp, Father?" you asked, your eyes wide with wonder. I told you it was to guide the lost and the weary, to keep them from the rocks that claimed so many lives. But I never told you how much I longed to guide you back to me.

I have watched the years pass like the waves, relentless and indifferent. The lighthouse stands as it always has, though its light has grown dim in my heart. I have kept the lamp burning, as I have kept your memory alive, though I do not know if it is for you or for myself.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that aches with the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long years since the last time we spoke, and I have not been the same since. The sea, once a source of solace and duty, now feels like a cruel adversary, and the lighthouse—my home, my refuge—seems to echo with the silence of your absence.

Tonight, the storm is upon us. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea rages with a fury that I fear may not be soothed. I have been watching the horizon for hours, and I do not believe I will see the dawn. The lantern has been lit, as it always is, but I wonder if it will be the last light to guide the ships that pass by.

**April 3rd, 1887**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit here by the great lamp, its glow flickering like the last embers of a dying fire. The sea howls beyond the cliffs, and the wind screams as though the very earth is weeping. I have not written to you in five years, and yet, tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to put pen to paper.

You were but a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with a heart as fierce as the tempests that rage upon these shores. I remember the day you stormed out of the house, your eyes blazing with a fire I could not extinguish. You called me a tyrant, a man who had no soul, and I let you go. I did not stop you. I did not beg you to stay. I did not even say goodbye.

And yet, I have not forgotten.

These years have passed like the tides, relentless and unrelenting. I have tended the light, kept the beacon burning through countless nights, and listened to the sea’s endless lullaby. I have watched the world change, and I have watched you fade from my life. I have seen your name in the newspapers, heard of your accomplishments, and I have said nothing. I have let time carry the weight of our silence.

I write not to beg, nor to plead, but to leave behind a final word—for you, and for myself.

I have not slept in days, and I fear that when the morning comes, I may not be here to greet it.
